data.title: Inter Action (TSE:7725) Q3 EPS Rebound Tests Bearish Margin Concerns
data.description:
Inter Action (TSE:7725) has put up another data point for investors to consider with its Q3 2026 numbers, reporting revenue of ¥1.57 billion and basic EPS of ¥26.70 as the latest snapshot of the year to date. The company has seen quarterly revenue move from ¥1.69 billion in Q2 2025 to ¥1.36 billion in Q4 2025, then to ¥1.05 billion in Q1 2026, ¥1.08 billion in Q2 2026 and ¥1.57 billion in Q3 2026. EPS shifted from ¥26.97 to ¥6.99, then ¥4.44, ¥17.43 and ¥26.70 over the same stretch, leaving investors to weigh these swings against a backdrop of softer margins and mixed earnings momentum.

See our full analysis for Inter Action.

With the headline figures set, the next step is to see how this earnings print lines up with the competing narratives around Inter Action's growth potential, profit quality and margin direction.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

TTM profit at ¥571.7 million on softer margins

  • Over the last twelve months to Q3 2026, Inter Action booked ¥5,065.4 million in revenue and ¥571.7 million in net income, with the trailing net profit margin at 11.3% compared with 18.2% a year earlier.
  • What stands out for a bullish view is that earnings quality is described as high and earnings are forecast to grow about 17.8% per year, yet this sits alongside the lower 11.3% trailing margin and a five year earnings decline of 4.6% annually, which creates tension between growth expectations and the recent profitability trend.
    • Forecast revenue growth of roughly 11.9% per year against the current ¥5,065.4 million trailing revenue supports the idea of a larger top line, while the margin step down from 18.2% to 11.3% keeps the focus on how much of that revenue turns into profit.
    • The most recent year showing negative earnings growth versus the five year average makes it harder for bulls to lean only on growth forecasts without also factoring in how margins have moved.

Curious how numbers like an 11.3% margin and ¥571.7 million of profit translate into real investing stories for this company? 📊 Read the what the Community is saying about Inter Action.

Q3 net income rebounds to ¥271 million

  • Within the current fiscal year, quarterly net income moved from ¥47.3 million in Q1 2026 to ¥176.7 million in Q2 and ¥271.0 million in Q3, alongside revenue of ¥1,048.6 million, ¥1,082.4 million and ¥1,570.0 million respectively.
  • For investors leaning toward a bullish interpretation, this run from ¥47.3 million to ¥271.0 million backs the idea of improving profit quality within the year, yet it still sits under the longer term context where trailing twelve month net income of ¥571.7 million is below the ¥979.0 million level seen a year earlier, so short term progress is being judged against a higher past base.
    • The Q3 2026 basic EPS of ¥26.70, up from ¥4.44 in Q1 and ¥17.43 in Q2, lines up with the higher quarterly profit, which may look supportive for those expecting the forecast 17.8% annual earnings growth.
    • At the same time, trailing EPS over the last twelve months is ¥54.56 compared with ¥89.43 a year earlier, so anyone taking the bullish side needs to reconcile the recent quarterly upswing with that lower trailing figure.

Mixed signals from P/E of 28.2x and DCF fair value

  • Inter Action trades on a trailing P/E of 28.2x, above the peer average of 24.3x and the Japan semiconductor industry at 25.9x, while the current share price of ¥1,587 sits about 44.4% below a DCF fair value of ¥2,854.67.
  • For a bearish reading, critics focus on the higher 28.2x P/E relative to peers and the industry and pair that with the drop in trailing net margin from 18.2% to 11.3%, even though the same data set shows the stock trading below the DCF fair value, which means the valuation picture depends heavily on whether an investor puts more weight on earnings multiples or on cash flow estimates.
    • The combination of a premium P/E and a five year earnings decline of 4.6% per year is often used to question how comfortable it is to pay more than peers for a company whose long term earnings record has not grown.
    • On the other hand, the DCF fair value of ¥2,854.67 versus the ¥1,587 market price, plus forecast earnings growth of about 17.8% per year, offers a counterpoint that valuation might look different when viewed through a discounted cash flow lens rather than just the current P/E.

Margins Under Pressure At 1.5%

  • On a trailing 12‑month basis, DaikokutenbussanLtd recorded a 1.5% net profit margin versus 2.4% the prior year, with Q3 2026 net income of ¥879 million on ¥80,017 million of revenue. This points to profitability that is currently tight for a food and discount retailer.
  • Critics highlight a bearish angle that a margin profile at 1.5% leaves little room for error, and the recent trend gives that view some support:
    • Trailing 12‑month net income declined from ¥6,901 million in the period ending Q3 2025 to ¥4,689 million in the period ending Q3 2026, while trailing revenue moved from ¥285,012 million to ¥314,075 million, so profit has not kept pace with sales.
    • Quarterly net income also sits below last year's levels, with ¥879 million in Q3 2026 compared with ¥1,757 million in Q3 2025. This lines up with the bearish concern that higher volumes are not yet translating into stronger bottom line results.

TTM EPS Slips To ¥342

  • Trailing 12‑month basic EPS is ¥342.11 for Q3 2026, down from ¥494.75 a year earlier, even though quarterly EPS within this year ranged between ¥51.47 and ¥80.73. As a result, the full year picture looks weaker than the recent single quarters alone.
  • Supporters of a more bullish stance point to the business model as a defensive consumer play, yet the earnings pattern adds some friction to that story:
    • Five‑year earnings growth averaged 4.4% annually and earnings quality is described as high, which fits the bullish view of a steady operator focused on essential goods. However, the most recent 12 months showed negative earnings growth despite trailing revenue moving from ¥292,940 million to ¥314,075 million.
    • Bulls also highlight strong earnings forecasts of about 20.25% per year, yet the step down from a trailing EPS of ¥485.99 at Q4 2025 to ¥342.11 at Q3 2026 suggests the starting point for that growth is currently softer than the bullish story alone would imply.

  • The shares trade on a trailing P/E of 14.2x, above peers at 11.8x and slightly above the Japan Consumer Retailing average of 13.7x. The current share price of ¥4,950 also sits well above the DCF fair value estimate of about ¥948.44.

However, the share price is more than 5x the DCF fair value estimate of ¥948.44, which bears see as a sign of stretched expectations.
